title-group.article-title: An updated systematic review and meta-analysis of modafinil for excessive daytime sleepiness in narcolepsy

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Neurology, Sant Parmanand Hospital, Delhi, India
bRollins School of Public Health, Emory University, Atlanta, GA, USA
cDepartment of Physiology and Biomedical Engineering, Mayo Clinic, Rochester, USA
dDow University of Health Sciences, Pakistan
ePramukhswami Medical College, Bhaikaka University, Karamsad, India
fMaharashtra University of Health Sciences, India

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Narcolepsy is a chronic disorder characterized by excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) and cataplexy, often triggered by stress, sleep disturbances, pregnancy, or trauma. Modafinil is a preferred treatment for EDS, known for improving quality of life, though long-term safety and efficacy data remain limited. This review was registered with PROSPERO (CRD420251136771).

sec.title: Aim
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the effectiveness of Modafinil in treating narcolepsy.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A comprehensive search of databases (PubMed, Embase, Google Scholar, Scopus, ScienceDirect, Web of Science) was conducted to identify cohort studies and randomized controlled trials (RCTs) up to August 2025. Efficacy outcomes included the Maintenance of Wakefulness Test (MWT) and Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS). Statistical analysis using Review Manager 5.4 compared Modafinil to placebo. The review aimed to reassess and synthesize existing randomized evidence rather than identify new efficacy trials.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Of 4567 articles screened, nine studies were included in the systematic review, and five studies, involving 997 adult patients, were analyzed in the meta-analysis. Modafinil significantly improved MWT scores (MD = 3.56, 95% CI [2.25–4.86]) and reduced ESS scores (MD = −3.34, 95% CI [–4.13 to −2.56]). Moderate heterogeneity was observed but was reduced following pre-specified sensitivity analyses.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: These findings confirm the short-term efficacy of modafinil in reducing excessive daytime sleepiness based on legacy randomized controlled trials conducted primarily in the 1990s and early 2000s, while highlighting the absence of new eligible RCTs in the past decade. However, long-term efficacy and safety remain to be established, underscoring the need for larger, long-duration randomized trials.

list-item.p: Modafinil significantly improves excessive daytime sleepiness in narcolepsy.
Meta-Analysis (n = 997) showed 3.56 min MWT gain and 3.34-point ESS reduction.
Split-dose regimens (400–600 mg) sustain wakefulness better than once-daily dosing.
Well tolerated overall; most adverse effects were mild and transient.
Evidence supports short-term efficacy; long-term safety remains underexplored.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Narcolepsy is a chronic neurological disorder that disrupts the sleep-wake cycle. It has a prevalence of approximately 0.05% and typically manifests within the first two decades of life [1], persisting throughout the patient's lifetime. The condition significantly affects the patient's quality of life, motor function, social interactions, occupational performance, mental health, and overall well-being, consequently impacting economic productivity, employment, daily functioning, and healthcare utilization [2]. Patients with Narcolepsy have an increased mortality rate across all age groups compared to the general population. However, this mortality difference is less pronounced in older age groups, where the death rate rises in both narcoleptic and non-narcoleptic individuals [3].

The onset of narcolepsy symptoms has been linked to significant psychological stress, major alterations in sleep-wake patterns, pregnancy, and trauma [4]. Narcolepsy is primarily characterized by Excessive Daytime Sleepiness (EDS) and cataplexy. EDS manifests as recurrent, irresistible episodes of daytime sleep, typically every 2 h, particularly during periods of physical inactivity, such as watching television or driving [4]. It is marked by the premature onset of Rapid Eye Movement (REM) sleep. In contrast, cataplexy, a hallmark of Narcolepsy, is the sudden, involuntary onset of skeletal muscle weakness or paralysis during wakefulness, triggered by emotional stimuli, especially positive emotions like joy or laughter, and less commonly by anger or frustration [4]. The disorder has a strong genetic association with the HLA DQB1*0602 allele [5,6]. Its pathogenesis is linked to reduced catecholamine levels, including norepinephrine, dopamine, and serotonin, in the brain, as well as hypersensitivity of cholinergic receptors [7].

Narcolepsy is diagnosed using the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM-5), and the International Classification of Sleep Disorders, Third Edition (ICSD-3) [6]. According to DSM-5 criteria, patients must experience EDS at least three times per week for the past three months, in addition to one of the following: 1) cataplexy; 2) cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) hypocretin deficiency; 3) REM sleep latency of ≤15 min on nocturnal polysomnography (PSG); or 4) a mean sleep latency of ≤8 min with ≥2 sleep-onset REM periods (SOREMPs) on Multiple Sleep Latency Testing (MSLT). ICSD-3 categorizes Narcolepsy into type 1 and type 2. Narcolepsy type 1 requires daily EDS for ≥3 months and one or both of the following: 1) cataplexy with mean sleep latency ≤8 min and ≥2 SOREMPs on MSLT; or SOREMP (≤15 min after sleep onset) on preceding PSG, which may replace one of the SOREMPs on MSLT; 2) low or absent CSF hypocretin-1 levels. Narcolepsy type 2 requires EDS and MSLT findings as described above, but without cataplexy, and CSF hypocretin-1 levels are either unknown or above the threshold for type 1. The hypersomnolence and MSLT findings must not be attributable to other conditions, such as insufficient sleep, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), delayed sleep phase disorder, or the effects of medications, substance use, or withdrawal.

The treatment of Narcolepsy includes both pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic approaches. Non-pharmacologic measures include maintaining good sleep hygiene and scheduling naps, which can help reduce unscheduled daytime sleep episodes [[8], [9], [10]]. However, most patients continue to rely on central nervous system (CNS) stimulants [10,11], and the evidence supporting naps alone for managing daytime sleepiness is limited [12]. A significant advancement in the pharmacotherapy of Narcolepsy occurred in the 1930s when CNS stimulants, particularly amphetamines, were introduced to manage symptoms [13,14]. Over time, several drug classes, including stimulants, antidepressants, and hypnotics, have been explored for narcolepsy treatment. Currently, the American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) strongly recommends modafinil, pitolisant, sodium oxybate, and solriamfetol, and makes conditional recommendations for armodafinil, methylphenidate, and dextroamphetamine. While all these agents reduce daytime sleepiness, only pitolisant, sodium oxybate, and dextroamphetamine have shown notable improvements in cataplexy [15].

Modafinil (2-[(diphenylmethyl)sulfinyl]acetamide) is a distinctive non-amphetamine CNS stimulant, first introduced in France in the 1980s for the treatment of central hypersomnias, including Narcolepsy [16]. Its mechanism of action has been debated; it was initially thought to function as a central alpha-adrenergic agonist [16]. However, further studies revealed that modafinil does not bind to adrenergic or serotonergic receptors even at high concentrations [17]. In vitro studies in rat and canine brains demonstrated that modafinil binds to dopamine transporters (DAT), inhibiting reuptake and increasing dopamine levels in the striatum [18]. These findings were supported by positron emission tomography (PET) studies in humans, which showed that modafinil decreases the binding of [11C]cocaine (a DAT radioligand) and [11C]raclopride (a D2/D3 radioligand) to endogenous receptors [19]. Before modafinil, amphetamines were the primary treatment for Narcolepsy, but modafinil became preferred due to its lower risk of tolerance, abuse, and disruption of nocturnal sleep compared to amphetamines [20]. Common side effects of modafinil include insomnia, nausea, diarrhea, headache, and dry mouth [16]. Modafinil is used as a racemic mixture of R- and S-enantiomers. The R-enantiomer has a half-life three to four times longer than the S-enantiomer [21]. The R-enantiomer, armodafinil, has a higher plasma concentration at the end of the day than modafinil, allowing once-daily dosing. In contrast, modafinil typically requires twice-daily administration [21].

Modafinil is preferred as a primary treatment for Narcolepsy due to its significant impact on reducing excessive daytime sleepiness, improving disease severity, and enhancing quality of life [16]. However, its effects on cataplexy remain inconclusive [8]. New alternatives to modafinil, such as flmodafinil/lauflumide and novel dopamine transporter inhibitor CE-123, are currently under investigation [22,23].

The existing evidence suggests that modafinil is a viable treatment option for effectively managing Narcolepsy with favorable tolerability. Our literature search was updated through August 2025. Based on our predefined inclusion criteria, we identified no eligible randomized controlled trials published after 2017, highlighting a persistent evidence gap. Accordingly, this systematic review and meta-analysis provide a methodologically updated and transparent synthesis of the existing randomized evidence, rather than new clinical efficacy data, to reassess the strength and limitations of modafinil in reducing EDS.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p:
This systematic review was registered with the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO registration number: CRD420251136771). This review focuses on clinical studies of modafinil use in patients with Narcolepsy. We excluded animal studies and publications that only discussed the pathophysiology of modafinil without presenting clinical data. This review was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA 2020) guidelines. The study selection process is summarized in Fig. 1 (PRISMA flow diagram), and the completed PRISMA 2020 checklist is available in Supplementary Table 2. As only data from published studies were used, ethical approval was not required.Fig. 1
PRISMA 2020 flow chart representing the screening and selection of studies. (PRISMA = Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis).Fig. 1

sec.title: Systematic literature search and study selection
sec.p: We conducted a thorough search for relevant publications using PubMed (including Medline), Embase, Google Scholar, Scopus, Web of Science, and ScienceDirect. We searched PubMed for studies cited in review papers, editorials, and commentaries. Nevertheless, we continued to search for additional studies that met our inclusion criteria.

We independently reviewed a list of abstracts for inclusion based on specific criteria, focusing on studies about modafinil and Narcolepsy. We excluded review papers and animal studies. Four reviewers conducted dual reviews, resolving disagreements through discussion.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p:
To meet the objectives of our study, we defined particular inclusion and exclusion criteria for participant selection. Our focus was solely on clinical trials and cohort studies. The PICOS framework (Population, Intervention, Comparison, and Outcome) guiding our selection was structured as follows:●
Population: adult patients diagnosed with Narcolepsy.●
Intervention: treatment with modafinil.●
Comparison: placebo or no intervention.●
Outcome: excessive daytime sleepiness symptoms were measured by the MWT and ESS.

Other criterias can be summarized in Table 1.Table 1
Inclusion and Exclusion criteria.Table 1
Inclusion CriteriaExclusion Criteria
Human StudiesAnimal Studies
From 1990 to 2025Only pathophysiology/methodological studies with no outcome data
English textNon-English text
Gender: AllAge: <18 years of age
Age: >18 years of agePapers that needed to be purchased
Free papersStudies involving clinical data other than Narcolepsy

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: The PICO criteria were utilized to conduct a thorough literature review. The search was conducted on databases such as PubMed (including Medline), Embase, Google Scholar, Scopus, Web of Science, and ScienceDirect, using relevant keywords, including Modafinil, Narcolepsy, and Wakefulness. The Medical Subject Heading (MeSH) approach, as used in PubMed (including Medline), Embase, Google Scholar, Scopus, Web of Science, and ScienceDirect, as detailed in Supplementary Table 1, was employed to develop a comprehensive search strategy.

In PubMed, the search string was (((Narcolepsy [MeSH Terms]) OR (Narcolepsy OR Excessive Daytime Sleepiness OR EDS OR Hypersomnia OR Sleep Disorder OR Daytime Sleepiness OR Somnolence OR Hypnolepsy OR Sleep Attacks)) AND ((Modafinil[MeSH Terms]) OR (Modafinil OR Provigil OR Alertec OR Modalert OR Modvigil OR Modiodal)) AND ((Armodafinil[MeSH Terms]) OR (Armodafinil OR Nuvigil OR Waklert OR Artvigil)) AND ((Wakefulness[MeSH Terms]) OR (Wakefulness OR Alertness OR Vigilance OR Awareness))). In Embase, the query used equivalent Emtree terms: ‘narcolepsy'/exp OR ‘excessive daytime sleepiness'/exp AND ‘modafinil'/exp AND ‘armodafinil'/exp AND 'wakefulness'/exp. The Scopus search applied the expression (TITLE-ABS-KEY(Narcolepsy*) AND TITLE-ABS-KEY(Modafinil*) AND TITLE-ABS-KEY(armodafinil*) AND TITLE-ABS-KEY(wakefulness)) with filters limited to English-language journal articles and reviews. The Science Direct query used (Narcolepsy OR “excessive daytime sleepiness”) AND Modafinil AND armodafinil AND (wakefulness OR alertness). In contrast, Web of Science used TS = (narcolepsy* OR “excessive daytime sleepiness*") AND TS = (Modafinil) AND TS = (armodafinil) AND TS = (wakefulness*). In Google Scholar, a broader search combined the terms “Armodafinil” OR “Modafinil” OR “Provigil” OR “Nuvigil” AND ("Narcolepsy” OR “Gelineau Syndrome”) AND “Wakefulness.” Filters were uniformly applied to restrict results to human studies, the English language, and adult participants (aged 18 years or older). All records retrieved from these databases were imported into Rayyan (Qatar Computing Research Institute) for screening and deduplication. Duplicates were automatically identified by matching titles, authors, and DOIs, and then manually verified before screening. Two reviewers independently screened all titles and abstracts, and any discrepancies were resolved by consensus. Inter-rater reliability was assessed using Cohen's kappa statistic within Rayyan to ensure consistency between reviewers. This comprehensive strategy was designed to maximize sensitivity and ensure retrieval of all relevant clinical studies evaluating Modafinil or armodafinil in Narcolepsy.

sec.title: Quality appraisal
sec.p:
To ensure the reliability of our chosen papers, we utilized various quality assessment tools. For the selected papers, we employed the PRISMA checklist and the Cochrane bias tool assessment for randomised clinical trials, systematic reviews, and meta-analyses, as shown in Fig. 2. Several older crossover trials were rated as having unclear risk in the “Other bias” domain due to limited reporting of methodological details, including washout periods, funding sources, and allocation concealment procedures. Given the age of these studies and the incomplete reporting standards at the time, these domains could not be confidently classified as low- or high-risk. This uncertainty was therefore conservatively rated as unclear risk rather than assumed bias.Fig. 2
Risk of bias summary for included studies assessed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool.Fig. 2

The “Other bias” domain reflects study-specific methodological uncertainties, including incomplete reporting of allocation concealment, unclear funding disclosures, and limited detail regarding crossover washout periods in older trials. (Green = Low risk; Yellow = Unclear risk; Red = High risk).

sec.title: Data extraction and outcome measures
sec.p: After the initial screening process, we gathered pertinent data using custom-designed extraction forms. Data extraction was conducted by three authors, with any discrepancies resolved by two additional authors. The collected information encompassed various aspects, including the study's methodology, design, modafinil dosage, participant demographics, levels of daytime sleepiness, and key findings (Supplementary Table 2). The primary efficacy outcomes were evaluated using the MWT and ESS scores.
MWT - higher MWT scores indicate a greater ability to remain awake [24].
ESS - a lower ESS score indicates less severity of EDS [25].

Although the MSLT was reported in several included trials, it was not meta-analyzed due to variability in reporting formats and limited availability of extractable, comparable continuous outcome data across studies. Therefore, MSLT findings were considered descriptively rather than quantitatively.

sec.title: Meta-analysis
sec.p: For data analysis, we utilized Review Manager (RevMan) software, version 5.4. Continuous data were expressed as the mean difference between the modafinil-treated group and the placebo, accompanied by a 95% confidence interval (CI). Statistical significance was defined by a p-value ≤0.05. Heterogeneity across studies was assessed using the I2 statistic and its corresponding p-value, interpreted in accordance with the Cochrane Handbook: 0–30% (low), 30–60% (moderate), 60–90% (substantial), and 75–100% (considerable) [26,27].

Initially, both fixed-effects and random-effects models were examined to evaluate the stability of pooled estimates. When low heterogeneity (I2 < 30%) was observed, the fixed-effects model yielded results that were consistent with those of the random-effects model. However, because included studies varied in design, sample size, dosing regimens, and follow-up duration, a random-effects model (DerSimonian–Laird method) was ultimately selected to account for potential inter-study variability and provide a more conservative, generalizable estimate of the pooled effect size. This approach aligns with Cochrane recommendations for meta-analyses of clinical trials expected to differ in population or methodology. Publication bias assessment was planned but not performed because fewer than ten studies were included per outcome, consistent with Cochrane guidance.

Sensitivity analyses were pre-specified to explore potential sources of heterogeneity related to study size, follow-up duration, and dosing variability. Larger and longer-duration trials were evaluated separately to assess their influence on pooled effect estimates and heterogeneity, consistent with Cochrane recommendations for exploratory robustness testing.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: After searching through six selected databases, PubMed (including Medline), Embase, Google Scholar, Scopus, Web of Science and ScienceDirect, we extracted 12801 articles. We then carefully reviewed each paper and applied specific criteria, which led to excluding 1125 articles. From the remaining 11676 papers, we chose not to utilize 11562 of them due to duplicates or unsatisfactory titles and abstracts. We closely examined the remaining 114 papers and excluded 105 more as their content did not meet our inclusion criteria.

Finally, we conducted a thorough quality check on the remaining nine papers, which all met our criteria. These nine articles are included in our final systematic review and five articles are included in meta-analysis. Table 2 provides a detailed description of each.Table 2
Summary of studies characteristics.Table 2
Author, yearCountryStudy DesignNumber of patientsInterventionFollow up durationConclusion
Moldofsky et al., 2000 [28]CanadaRandomised Double BlindCrossover study63Modafinil 500 mg daily dose during open label and were continued with either modafinil or placebo during double blind period.24 weeksThis study demonstrated that modafinil is effective and well-tolerated in the long-term treatment of EDS in narcoleptic patients. Over a 16-week open-label period, followed by a 2-week randomized, double-blind phase, patients on modafinil (mean dose 330 mg) showed a significant reduction in EDS. The MWT revealed a 70% longer sleep latency in the modafinil group compared to placebo. Similarly, the ESS scores were lower in the modafinil group, and episodes of severe somnolence and sleep were reduced. Importantly, no significant effects on nocturnal sleep, blood pressure, heart rate, ECG, or mood were observed, confirming the continued efficacy and safety of modafinil over the treatment period.
Billiard et al., 1994 [29]FranceRandomized controlled, double blinded50Administration of modafinil 300 mg12 weeksThe results showed no significant change in nighttime sleep duration, wake time, or number of awakenings. However, daytime sleepiness episodes significantly decreased, and total daytime sleep time reduced. No changes were noted in cataplexy or feelings upon awakening. Modafinil improved EDS as measured by the MWT. Side effects were lower with modafinil compared to placebo.
Boivin et al., 1993 [30]FranceRandomized Double blind crossover study104 weeks of either modafinil 200 mg morning + 100 mg noon or placebo12 weeksThe clinical evaluation by the sleep-disorder physician indicated that modafinil produced nonsignificant alerting effects. There was no change in the daily number of cataplectic attacks after modafinil treatment compared to placebo. However, modafinil significantly reduced the number of gaps and the percentage of errors on the Four-Choice Reaction Time Test (FCRTT) compared to placebo. Although a reduction in mean reaction time was observed during modafinil treatment, this change did not reach statistical significance. Modafinil did not produce any changes in nocturnal sleep parameters. Eight out of 10 narcoleptic patients had a pathological Periodic Limb Movement (PLM) index (>5). A nonsignificant reduction in both the number and index of PLMs was observed with modafinil compared to placebo.
Schwartz et al., 2003 [31]USARandomised Double Blind Crossover study32Modafinil 400 mg once daily/400 mg split in split doses/200 mg once daily.3 weeksThe study showed that split-dose regimens (400 mg and 600 mg) were significantly more effective in maintaining wakefulness in the late afternoon/evening compared to the 200 mg once-daily regimen. After 3 weeks, 60% of patients on the 400 mg split-dose regimen and 58% on the 600 mg split-dose regimen could stay awake for at least 20 min in the evening, compared to just 9% of patients on the 200 mg once-daily regimen. All regimens were well tolerated, with mild or moderate adverse events reported in 18% of participants.
Broughton et al., 1997 [32]CanadaRandomised Double Blind Crossover study75Placebo vs modafinil 200 mg vs modafinil 400 mg divided dose.6 weeksThe study found that modafinil effectively reduces EDS in patients with narcolepsy. Compared to placebo, the 200 mg and 400 mg doses increased sleep latency by 40% and 54%, respectively, on the MWT. Both doses significantly reduced the likelihood of falling asleep during daily activities, as measured by the ESS, with a reduction of 24% for 200 mg and 26% for 400 mg in involuntary sleep episodes and severe somnolence. Patients preferred modafinil over placebo, with 84% choosing modafinil as their best treatment, and side effects were more frequent with the 400 mg dose. The 200 mg dose was equally effective with fewer side effects, making it a well-tolerated option.
Fry et al., 1997 [33]USARandomized controlled, double blinded285Modafinil (400 mg) 100 mg × 4 tablets daily or a placebo were given to participants during the double-blind phase.9 weeksThe study showed that modafinil significantly improved subjective and objective measures of sleepiness, including the ESS and the MSLT. The mean sleep latency increased by up to 4 min in modafinil groups compared to placebo, and Clinical Global Impression scores also improved significantly in modafinil groups. Adverse effects were dose-dependent but mostly mild to moderate, with headache being the most common. The study demonstrated that modafinil is an effective and well-tolerated treatment for EDS in narcolepsy, with sustained efficacy over long-term use​.
Gross et al., 2000 [34]USARandomized controlled, double blinded271Modafinil 200 mg once daily/modafinil 400 mg once daily/placebo9 weeksThe study showed that both dosages of modafinil significantly improved objective measures of EDS, as indicated by the MSLT and MWT. Specifically, the modafinil 400 mg group showed a mean sleep latency increase to 5.1 min compared to 3.5 min in the placebo group, and the MWT sleep latency was significantly extended at every follow-up visit. Subjective sleepiness also improved as reflected by the ESS. The treatment was well tolerated, with headache being the most common adverse effect, but its incidence was not significantly higher than that in the placebo group. The study concluded that modafinil is an effective and safe treatment option for managing EDS in narcolepsy, with no evidence of dependence or withdrawal symptoms over the treatment duration.
Schwartz et al., 2005 [35]USARandomized controlled, double blinded56Varying doses of modafinil (200 mg, 400 mg, 600 mg) in either once-daily or split-dose regimens.3 weeksThe study showed that split-dose regimens (400 mg and 600 mg) were significantly more effective in maintaining wakefulness in the late afternoon/evening compared to the 200 mg once-daily regimen. After 3 weeks, 60% of patients on the 400 mg split-dose regimen and 58% on the 600 mg split-dose regimen could stay awake for at least 20 min in the evening, compared to just 9% of patients on the 200 mg once-daily regimen. All regimens were well tolerated, with mild or moderate adverse events reported in 18% of participants.
Dauvilliers et al., 2017 [36]USARandomized controlled, double blinded155Sodium oxybate 9 g nightly/modafinil 200-600 mg once daily/their combination8 weeksThe post hoc analysis of polysomnography data from 155 patients revealed that SXB, alone or with modafinil, significantly reduced shifts from deep sleep stages (N2/3/REM) to light sleep or wakefulness. Sleep quality, measured using the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index, also improved significantly with SXB and SXB + modafinil, but not with modafinil alone. These results suggest that SXB has a specific effect on consolidating sleep and improving sleep quality, while modafinil alone had minimal impact on Disrupted nighttime sleep (DNS)​.

sec.title: MWT
sec.p:
Five studies involving 750 patients utilized the MWT scale for outcome measurement. There was a significant increase in the MWT scale in the modafinil treated group compared to placebo (MD = 3.56, 95% CI [2.25 to 4.86], p < 0.00001). Substantial heterogeneity was found across studies (I2 = 67%, p = 0.02) (Fig. 3). In pre-specified sensitivity analyses, exclusion of larger and longer-duration trials (Gross et al. and Moldofsky et al.) reduced heterogeneity to I2 = 23%, without materially altering the pooled effect estimate [27,28,34].Fig. 3
Forest plot showing the mean difference in MWT scores between the Modafinil and placebo groups. Sensitivity analyses were pre-specified in the study protocol and conducted as exploratory assessments to investigate heterogeneity rather than to selectively influence pooled results. (MWT = Maintenance of Wakefulness Test).Fig. 3

sec.title: ESS
sec.p:
Four studies involving 690 patients utilized the ESS scale for outcome measurement. There was a significant reduction in the ESS in the modafinil treated group compared to placebo (MD = −3.34, 95% CI [−4.13 to −2.56], p < 0.00001). Moderate heterogeneity was found across studies (I2 = 43%, p = 0.15) (Fig. 4). Sensitivity analysis excluding Fry et al. reduced heterogeneity to I2 = 18% (p = 0.29) (Supplementary Fig. 2), confirming that heterogeneity was not statistically significant (p > 0.05) [27,33].Fig. 4
Forest plot showing the mean difference in ESS scores between the Modafinil and placebo groups. Sensitivity analyses were pre-specified in the study protocol and conducted as exploratory assessments to investigate heterogeneity rather than to selectively influence pooled results. (ESS = Epworth Sleepiness Scale).Fig. 4

The observed heterogeneity may partly reflect variation in dosing regimens (200–600 mg/day, single versus split doses). Sensitivity analysis excluding higher-dose studies (≥600 mg) did not materially change the pooled effect, suggesting that modafinil's efficacy is consistent across commonly used dosing ranges.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Narcolepsy is a chronic neurological disorder with profound social, economic, and health impacts [25]. The condition significantly impairs daily functioning, with EDS and cataplexy leading to difficulties in maintaining employment, education, and personal relationships [37]. Individuals often face social isolation, stigma, and reduced quality of life due to their inability to control sleep episodes. Narcolepsy also places a substantial burden on healthcare systems, increasing costs associated with frequent medical visits and treatments. Effective management and increased public awareness are crucial to improving both social integration and clinical outcomes for affected individuals [38].

Modafinil, a non-amphetamine CNS stimulant, plays a pivotal role in managing EDS in Narcolepsy. It enhances wakefulness by inhibiting dopamine reuptake through binding to dopamine transporters, thereby increasing dopamine levels in the brain [39]. Compared to traditional stimulants like amphetamines, Modafinil has a lower risk of tolerance, abuse, and disruption of nocturnal sleep [40]. Furthermore, it is well-tolerated, with common side effects including headache, nausea, and insomnia [41]. Owing to its efficacy in improving EDS, Modafinil is a first-line treatment for Narcolepsy and significantly improves patients' quality of life, though its effects on cataplexy remain inconclusive [42]. Additionally, its once-daily formulation, armodafinil, offers similar benefits with longer-lasting effects [43].

In our systematic review and meta-analysis, we assessed the efficacy of modafinil for the treatment of narcolepsy, with a focus on EDS. It is important to emphasize that the randomized evidence supporting modafinil is largely derived from trials conducted in the 1990s and early 2000s. Despite an updated literature search extending through August 2025, no new eligible randomized controlled trials have been published in the past decade. The absence of new randomized trials over the past decade, despite continued reliance on modafinil in clinical practice, underscores a critical gap in long-term and comparative effectiveness evidence rather than a lack of therapeutic relevance. Accordingly, the present findings should be interpreted as confirming established short-term efficacy rather than reflecting recent clinical trial activity. Within these limitations, analysis of nine studies encompassing 997 patients, including five studies with 750 patients in the quantitative synthesis, demonstrated significant improvements in EDS as measured by the es and ESS.

Multiple studies, including systematic reviews and meta-analyses, have consistently demonstrated the efficacy of modafinil in treating EDS in narcolepsy, with significant improvements in wakefulness, attention, and overall functioning relative to placebo, and generally good tolerability [44]. Systematic reviews further highlight modafinil's superiority over traditional stimulants, such as amphetamines, due to its lower abuse potential and fewer side effects [41]. While modafinil effectively reduces EDS, its impact on cataplexy remains limited, necessitating adjunct therapies for comprehensive symptom management. Modafinil remains a cornerstone in narcolepsy treatment, enhancing quality of life and daily functioning [43], though further well-controlled studies are needed to establish its long-term safety and efficacy.

Building on these earlier syntheses, the present review strengthens confidence in the current evidence base by explicitly applying PRISMA 2020 reporting standards, conducting a Cochrane risk-of-bias assessment, and performing prespecified sensitivity analyses to reduce heterogeneity and verify the robustness of pooled results. Together, these established methods, applied transparently to the most up-to-date dataset, ensure our conclusions accurately reflect the current strength and limitations of the evidence on Modafinil.

The pooled mean difference of +3.56 min in the Maintenance of Wakefulness Test (MWT) indicates that modafinil offers a meaningful benefit for sustained wakefulness in patients with narcolepsy. This improvement is clinically significant, as prior research shows that a 2–3-min increase in MWT can enhance daytime functioning and driving performance. The observed 3.34-point reduction in Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS) scores further confirms a clinically relevant decrease in subjective sleepiness. Together, these findings provide clear evidence that modafinil significantly improves both objective and subjective aspects of wakefulness in narcolepsy.

Compared with other meta-analyses, our results align with previous findings, demonstrating significant improvements in MWT and ESS with modafinil versus placebo [37,38]. Real-world data similarly support its effectiveness, reporting improvements in daily productivity and reduced risk of sleep-related accidents [39]. Mechanistically, modafinil primarily mediates its wake-promoting effects by inhibiting DAT, increasing extracellular dopamine concentrations in wakefulness-related regions, such as the striatum and nucleus accumbens, consistent with updated human DAT structural insights [45]. PET studies confirm that modafinil administration reduces [11C]-cocaine and [11C]-raclopride binding, which supports its dopaminergic modulation without the high abuse potential seen with amphetamines [18,39]. This DAT inhibition likely underlies the improved wake maintenance observed in our pooled analysis.

Our pooled analysis of the included studies showed a significant increase in the MWT score and a decrease in ESS scores in the modafinil-treated group compared to the placebo group. These findings align with contemporary evidence across wake-promoting agents (WPAs), including network meta-analyses and long-term extension data [[46], [47], [48]]. Significant heterogeneity among studies, due to variable methodologies and dosing regimens (200–600 mg/day), was observed but was mitigated by sensitivity analysis and the use of a random-effects model, supporting consistent benefit across dosing ranges and populations.

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: We employed a comprehensive search strategy across multiple databases, including PubMed, Embase, and Scopus, to ensure we included a broad range of relevant studies. Using standardized tools such as PRISMA 2020 and the Cochrane bias tool, we enhanced the study's methodological transparency and rigor. We conducted sensitivity analyses to account for heterogeneity arising from variations in study design and dosing regimens, thereby strengthening the validity of the pooled estimates. While we did not introduce new efficacy signals, our strength lies in the transparent re-evaluation of legacy trials using contemporary methodological standards and in highlighting the stagnation of randomized evidence in this area.

However, several limitations should be acknowledged. First, publication and selection biases cannot be excluded, as only English-language, freely accessible studies were included. This approach, while practical, may have excluded high-quality non-English or subscription-based trials, potentially skewing results toward more favorable findings. Second, inconsistencies in diagnostic criteria across the included trials, some using the ICSD-2 and others using the ICSD-3, may have led to subtle differences in participant classification, particularly regarding narcolepsy types 1 and 2. Although the diagnostic framework has evolved from ICSD-2 to ICSD-3, distinguishing Narcolepsy into type 1 and type 2, this reclassification does not alter therapeutic decisions regarding Modafinil. Because excessive daytime sleepiness remains the cardinal feature across both subtypes, Modafinil remains recommended as first-line therapy, irrespective of classification [15,43]. Third, variability in dosing strategies (200–600 mg/day, single vs. split regimens) and follow-up durations (3-24 weeks) introduces clinical heterogeneity that limits comparability. Additionally, most included trials had small sample sizes and short-term follow-up, constraining conclusions on long-term efficacy and safety. Efficacy estimates reflect short-term RCTs; durability and long-term safety require cautious interpretation despite supportive extension data [47,48]. Formal publication bias testing (e.g., funnel plots or Egger's regression) was not conducted because fewer than 10 studies were available for each outcome, which limits the reliability of such analyses, as recommended by the Cochrane Handbook. Few studies assessed cataplexy or quality-of-life outcomes, which remain clinically significant dimensions of treatment response.

sec.title: Future directions
sec.p: Future research should prioritize long-term safety studies and head-to-head comparisons between modafinil, armodafinil, solriamfetol, and emerging wake-promoting agents, ideally designed in collaboration with hypersomnia and sleep-medicine experts. Direct head-to-head comparisons and real-world cohort studies are also needed to inform personalized therapy for EDS and to evaluate outcomes beyond alertness, including cognitive performance and psychosocial functioning. Emerging orexin-2 receptor agonists show large short-term improvements in narcolepsy type 1 but raise safety questions requiring longer follow-up. Head-to-head or adaptive trials against established WPAs would further clarify their clinical positioning [49].

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Govind Singh Mann: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Manju Ramakrishnan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Madona L. Pakkam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation. Nimra Sufiyan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Romil Kukadiya: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources. Khushal B. Girigosavi: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: As this study analyzed data from previously published research, ethical approval was not required.

sec.title: Funding declaration
sec.p: No funding was received for this research.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
